content.Title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
Organizations: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute (NHLBI)
content.Purpose: To translate scientific evidence into clinical practice guidelines to improve cardiovascular health through prevention, detection, evaluation, and management of high blood pressure (BP) in adults.
content.Development: Guidelines were developed by the ACC/AHA Task Force with collaborative input from national organizations, professional societies, and members.
content.Clinical Implementation: Management in accordance with guideline recommendations is effective only when followed by both practitioners and patients. Shared decision making between clinicians and patients is emphasized.
content.Methodology and Modernization: The guidelines are continuously reviewed, updated, and modified based on published standards and technological advancements. The 'modular knowledge chunk format' is introduced to facilitate updates as new evidence becomes available.
content.Intended Use: Practice guidelines provide recommendations applicable to patients with or at risk of developing cardiovascular disease (CVD).

content.Guideline Overview: The guideline emphasizes cost-value considerations for drugs, devices, or interventions where appropriate. It involves continuous review and potential updates based on new evidence. The Task Force aims to avoid biases by selecting experts from diverse backgrounds and has strict policies on industry relationships.
content.Evidence Review: The writing committee uses evidence-based methodologies primarily from randomized controlled trials. There is a mechanism for commissioning independent evidence review committees (ERC) when needed.
content.Guideline-Directed Management: This term includes clinical evaluation, diagnostic testing, and treatments, with recommendations bound to drugs and devices approved for use in the U.S.
content.Class of Recommendation: The Class of Recommendation (COR) and Level of Evidence (LOE) are used to determine the strength and quality of guideline recommendations.

content.title: Applying Class of Recommendation and Level of Evidence to Clinical Strategies, Interventions, Treatments, or Diagnostic Testing in Patient Care (Updated August 2015)

classes_of_recommendation.Class I (Strong): Benefit >>> Risk. Recommendations should be performed/administered.
classes_of_recommendation.Class III: No Benefit (Moderate): Benefit = Risk. Recommendations are not useful/effective.
classes_of_recommendation.Class III: Harm (Strong): Risk > Benefit. Recommendations are potentially harmful.

levels_of_evidence.Level A: High-quality evidence from multiple RCTs or meta-analyses.
levels_of_evidence.Level B-R: Moderate-quality evidence from one or more RCTs.
levels_of_evidence.Level B-NR: Moderate-quality evidence from well-designed nonrandomized studies.
levels_of_evidence.Level C-LD: Evidence from observational studies with limitations.

notes: COR and LOE are independent; any COR can pair with any LOE.
A recommendation with LOE C does not imply weakness.
RCTs may not be available for all clinical questions.
Outcome or intervention result should specify clinical outcome or diagnostic accuracy.

content.introduction: The guideline discusses the development of effective treatment for high blood pressure (BP) starting from the 1920s and significantly in the 1959 Build and Blood Pressure Study. It highlights the relationship between BP levels and the risk of clinical complications and death. Subsequent findings and reports have led to the development of comprehensive guidelines for detection, evaluation, and management of high BP. The current guideline aims to update prior Joint National Committee (JNC) reports.
content.methodology: The evidence review involved an extensive examination of literature from various databases conducted between February 2014 and August 2015. The search encompassed multiple terms related to high BP, medication, therapy, and associated conditions.
content.systematic review: A systematic review focusing on 4 clinical questions related to hypertension was conducted. The details of these questions are summarized in Table 2.
content.organization: The writing committee consisted of various healthcare professionals and no financial conflicts of interest were reported. The guideline aligns with the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline for hypertension management.

content.scope_of_guideline: Resource for clinical and public health practice communities. Focus on prevention, detection, evaluation, and management of high BP. Updated from the NHBLI publication on high BP.
content.BP_risk_information: Includes new information on CVD risk, ABPM, HBPM, telemedicine, etc. Excludes BP-lowering medication usage for prevention of SIHD and HF in absence of hypertension.
content.BP_CVD_risk: Observational studies show graded associations between BP and increased CVD risk.

content.summary: The document discusses the relationship between blood pressure components and cardiovascular disease (CVD) outcomes. Increased systolic and diastolic blood pressures are associated with higher CVD risk. Population risk data highlight hypertension as a leading cause of death and disability. The guideline recommends screening and managing CVD risk factors in adults with hypertension.
Key Points: Higher SBP and DBP are linked to increased risks of CVD and related complications.
Increased CVD risk associated with high SBP and DBP has been observed across a wide age spectrum.
Managing other modifiable CVD risk factors in adults with hypertension is recommended.
Hypertension is a significant risk factor for multiple severe conditions such as heart disease, stroke, and end-stage renal disease.

content.Synopsis: Many adult patients with hypertension also have other cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factors. These include smoking, obesity, diabetes mellitus (DM), and chronic kidney disease (CKD). With hypertension, estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is less than 60 mL/min/1.73 m², and there may be albumin to creatinine ratios of 300 mg/g or higher. U.S. adults with diagnosed DM often have hypertension, wherein the Chronic Renal Insufficiency Cohort shows 86% hypertension prevalence. Modifiable risk factors like smoking, dyslipidemia, obesity, and physical inactivity are highlighted. The article stresses the need to address multiple CVD risk factors to reduce high blood pressure (BP) through various mechanisms.
content.Recommendation for Definition of High BP: BP should be categorized as normal, elevated, or stage 1 or 2 hypertension to prevent and treat high BP.

content.clinical_guideline: This clinical guideline from the 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline discusses new definitions for hypertension stages based on observational data related to blood pressure (BP) and cardiovascular disease (CVD) risks. Stage 1 hypertension is defined as a systolic blood pressure (SBP) of 130-139 mm Hg or diastolic blood pressure (DBP) of 80-89 mm Hg. Stage 2 hypertension is defined as an SBP ≥140 mm Hg or DBP ≥90 mm Hg. The guideline addresses the long-term risk of hypertension and its prevalence, highlighting racial disparities in hypertension risk. Recommendations emphasize lifestyle modification and pharmacological intervention for managing elevated BP and hypertension stages.
content.BP_classification_system: The BP classification system is used to predict cardiovascular risks and assess intervention needs. Normal BP is defined as SBP <120 mm Hg and DBP <80 mm Hg. Elevated BP is SBP 120-129 mm Hg and DBP <80 mm Hg. Hypertension Stage 1 is SBP 130-139 mm Hg or DBP 80-89 mm Hg, and Stage 2 is SBP ≥140 mm Hg or DBP ≥90 mm Hg.
content.prevalence_estimates: Prevalence rates are estimated based on different cutoff points and vary with the racial and age demographic. The guideline provides estimates for race-specific long-term risk projections and suggests that the U.S. adult population prevalence may vary with the guideline criterion used.

content.Prevalence of hypertension is higher in: age groupsions with prevalence data adjusted for age. Treatment includes lifestyle modifications and medication, while control measures the achievement of satisfactory SBP/DBP.

content.guideline_summary: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline emphasizes the importance of accurate BP measurement for diagnosis and management of high BP. It outlines a protocol for BP measurement, including proper patient preparation, device validation, and technique.
BP_measurement_recommendations: Have the patient relax for more than 5 minutes before measuring.
Avoid caffeine, exercise, and smoking 30 minutes prior.
Average multiple readings to estimate BP.
BP_measurement_methods: Auscultatory: using mercury columns for direct measurement.
Oscillometric: involving sensors to detect changes in blood volume.
clinical_issues_addressed: Errors in BP measurement leading to misleading BP levels.
Structured BP treatment strategies have shown improvement in BP control.
content.evidence_reference: Structured, goal-oriented BP treatment initiatives improve BP control, with higher control rates in specialized settings.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: ABPM is used to obtain out-of-office BP readings at set intervals over a period of 24 hours, whereas HBPM is used at regular intervals by the patient. These methods provide better estimates based on multiple measurements.
Synopsis: Out-of-office measurement of BP can be helpful for confirmation and management of hypertension. Self-monitoring is usually less effective without interventions. ABPM is accepted as the best method but HBPM may be more practical. HBPM should be validated by an internationally accepted protocol.

Patient Training: Acknowledgment that individual BP readings may vary substantially
Devices: Verify use of automated validated devices.
Check for significant left/right inter-arm differences in BP.
Instructions on HBPM Procedures: Ensure proper sitting posture during measurements.
Bottom of the cuff should be placed directly above the antecubital fossa.
Procedures for Use of HBPM.Take Multiple Readings: At least 2 readings 1 min apart in the morning and evening.
Record All Readings: Monitors with built-in memory should be brought to all clinic appointments.
BP should be based on an average of readings on ≥2 occasions for clinical decision-making.

Values of SBP/DBP for Clinic and ABPM Me: HBPM, Daytime ABPM, Nighttime ABPM, 24-hour ABPM values provided.

Ambulatory BP Monitoring.Major RCTs: Based on the use of clinic BP readings supplemented by ABPM
Ambulatory BP Monitoring.ABPM Conducted: Includes readings every 15 to 30 minutes throughout the day, and every 15 minutes to 1 hour during the night.

content.ABPM and HBPM Definitions: ABPM (Ambulatory Blood Pressure Monitoring) and HBPM (Home Blood Pressure Monitoring) use different BP thresholds than office-based approaches. ABPM offers measurements throughout the day and night, while HBPM provides periodic readings.
content.BP Thresholds: Clinic BP of 140/90 mm Hg corresponds to home BP of 135/85 mm Hg and ABPM of 130/80 mm Hg over 24 hours.
content.Masked and White Coat Hypertension: White coat hypertension is identified when BP readings are higher in a medical setting but normal at home. Masked hypertension occurs when normal office readings mask higher readings elsewhere.
content.Methodological Issues: Studies vary in BP monitoring methods, including daytime and nighttime readings and the use of ABPM and HBPM.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In adults with untreated SBP greater than 130 mm Hg but less than 160 mm Hg or DBP greater than 80 mm Hg but less than 100 mm Hg, screening for white coat hypertension is reasonable.
Adults with white coat hypertension should undergo periodic monitoring with ABPM or HBPM.
In adults treated for hypertension with off-target office BP readings, ABPM confirmation for white coat effect is recommended.
In untreated adults with office BPs between 120/129 mm Hg SBP or between 75/79 mm Hg DBP, HBPM screening for masked hypertension is reasonable.

content.synopsis: Noninvasive BP monitoring differentiates hypertension categories by measurement place: white coat hypertension (elevated office BP but normal out-of-office ABPM/HBPM), masked hypertension (normal office BP but elevated out-of-office ABPM/HBPM), and sustained hypertension (elevated in both). In treated patients, 'white coat effect' (higher office than out-of-office BP) and 'masked uncontrolled hypertension' (controlled office but uncontrolled out-of-office BP) have been reported. White coat effect often defined as office SBP/DBP >20/10 mm Hg higher than home/ABPM.

prevalence_and_risk.masked_hypertension_prevalence: 10% to 26% (mean ~13%) in population-based surveys; 14% to 30% in nonnormotensive clinic populations
prevalence_and_risk.masked_hypertension_risk: Risk of CVD and all-cause mortality in masked hypertension is similar to sustained hypertension and about twice that of normotensive counterparts
prevalence_and_risk.white_coat_prevalence_factors: Prevalence of white coat hypertension increases with age, female sex, nonsmoking status, and routine office measurement by clinicians
prevalence_and_risk.white_coat_risk: Many studies show minimal increase in risk of CVD complications or all-cause mortality in white coat hypertension, though data vary

clinical_implications_and_recommendation.screening: Recommendation by some panels to screen for white coat hypertension with ABPM or HBPM to avoid unnecessary antihypertensive drug initiation
clinical_implications_and_recommendation.treatment_data: No definitive data on risks and benefits of treating white coat or masked hypertension, but masked and masked uncontrolled hypertension are associated with increased target organ damage and higher risk of CVD, stroke, and mortality compared with normotensive individuals
clinical_implications_and_recommendation.office_measurement_consideration: Routine office BP measurement by clinician observers tends to show higher white coat prevalence compared with unattended measurements

content.figure_1_detection_algorithm_summary: Algorithm for patients not on drug therapy: If office BP elevated (e.g., ≥130/80 or 120–129/<80 depending on pathway) after lifestyle modification, perform daytime ABPM or HBPM. If out-of-office BP is normal -> White Coat Hypertension: recommend lifestyle modification and annual ABPM/HBPM. If out-of-office BP elevated -> Masked Hypertension or Hypertension: continue lifestyle modification and initiate antihypertensive drug therapy as indicated. Colors correspond to class of recommendation.

content.guideline_title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.detection_focus: Detection of white coat effect or masked uncontrolled hypertension in patients on drug therapy.
content.algorithm: The flowchart outlines a process for evaluating patients with suspected white coat effect or masked uncontrolled hypertension based on office BP, home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM), and ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM).

process.action: Screen for increased CVD risk or target organ damage. If no risk, further screening unnecessary.

process.scenario: Office BP ≥5–10 mm Hg above goal on ≥3 agents
process.action: Screen for white coat effect with HBPM.

outcomes: White coat effect: Confirm with ABPM.
content.importance: The guideline emphasizes the detection of white coat hypertension as it can slightly increase cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk and overall mortality.

Hypertension Causes.Genetic Predisposition: Hypertension is influenced by genetic factors, including more than 25 rare mutations and 120 single-nucleotide polymorphisms. These account for about 3.5% of BP variability.
Hypertension Causes.Environmental Risk Factors: Diet, physical activity, and alcohol consumption impact BP. Overweight, obesity, excess sodium, low potassium, calcium, and magnesium, and alcohol use are associated with high BP.

Clinical Guidance.White Coat Effect: Can lead to underestimation of BP in office settings, necessitating out-of-office measurements.
Clinical Guidance.Masked Hypertension: Similar cardiovascular risk as sustained hypertension, necessitating close monitoring with out-of-office readings.
Clinical Guidance.Treatment Implications: Confirmation of BP control through ABPM or HBPM is recommended over office measurements.

genes.context: The current tabulation of known genetic variants contributing to BP and hypertension includes more than 25 rare mutations and 120 single-nucleotide polymorphisms.

Body Mass Index.relationship: Continuous and almost linear relationship with BP, indicating higher BMI strongly related to hypertension risk.
specific studies: Nurses’ Health Study: reported 40% hypertension rate by obesity.
Framingham Offspring Study: showed 78% in men and 65% in women, with a relative risk of 2.7 associated with obesity.

Sodium Intake.effects: Consistently associated with BP in various studies. Excessive consumption increases stroke and CVD risk independently.
Sodium Intake.salt sensitivity: High in certain groups, influencing BP independently.

Potassium Intake.relationship with BP: Inversely related to BP and stroke incidence. Higher levels seem to depress sodium's effects.

Physical Fitness.relationship: Improves BP and reduces hypertension risk. Physical fitness in young adults predicts future lower hypertension risk.

Alcohol Consumption.relationship: Direct association with BP noted since 1915. Increasing alcohol intake typically elevates hypertension and impacts cholesterol levels.

content.Relationship of childhood BP to adult BP: A meta-analysis of 50 studies shows correlation coefficients of about 0.38 for SBP and 0.28 for DBP. Factors like genetic factors and obesity increase the likelihood of high childhood BP leading to future hypertension.
content.Premature birth effects: Associated with a 4-mm Hg higher SBP and a 3-mm Hg higher DBP in adulthood, with larger effects in women.

Recommendations: Screening for specific forms of secondary hypertension is recommended when clinical indications and physical examination findings are present or in adults with resistant hypertension.
If an adult screens positive for a form of secondary hypertension, referral to a specialist for diagnostic confirmation and treatment may be reasonable.

content.Secondary hypertension: Specific cause can be identified in 10% of adult patients. Diagnosis and treatment can improve BP control and reduce CVD risk.

Conditions: Disproportionate target organ damage for degree of hypertension
Onset of diastolic hypertension in older adults (age ≥65 y)

Positive Screening Test.Yes: Refer to clinician with specific expertise (Class IIb)

Clinical Indications: Family history of polycystic kidney disease
Renal Parenchymal Disease.Additional/Confirmatory Tests: Tests to evaluate cause of renal disease

Clinical Indications: Hypertension of abrupt onset or worsening
Early-onset hypertension, especially in women (fibromuscular hyperplasia)

content.summary: This section discusses numerous substances, including prescription medications, over-the-counter medications, herbals, and food substances, that may affect blood pressure (BP) control. It details the importance of a careful history in patients with hypertension to manage substances that might impair BP control, and emphasizes reducing or discontinuing such substances.
