Primary Aldosteronism.Definition: A group of disorders where aldosterone production is inappropriately high for sodium status, autonomous of major regulators.
Primary Aldosteronism.Prevalence: 5% to 10% in patients with hypertension; 20% in patients with resistant hypertension
Diagnosis: Screening in presence of resistant hypertension, hypokalemia, incidentally discovered adrenal mass, family history of early-onset hypertension, or stroke at a young age (<40 years).
Use of plasma aldosterone: renin activity ratio.

Recommendations: In adults with hypertension, screening for primary aldosteronism is recommended in certain conditions.
Use of the plasma aldosterone: renin activity ratio for screening.
Referral to a hypertension specialist or endocrinologist for further evaluation and treatment if positive screening.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Medical therapy is recommended for adults with atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis.

In adults with renal artery stenosis for whom medical management has failed (refractory hypertension, worsening renal function, and/or intractable HF) and those with nonatherosclerotic disease, including fibromuscular dysplasia, it may be reasonable to refer the patient for consideration of revascularization.

Key Points: Primary aldosteronism is suggested by hypertension with increased plasma or renin activity.
Aldosterone-renin activity ratio is used for screening primary aldosteronism.
Confirmatory testing is often required for primary aldosteronism diagnosis.
Adrenal venous sampling is important for treatment planning in confirmed cases.
Treatments include mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists or adrenalectomy.

content.Synopsis: Obstructive sleep apnea is a chronic condition marked by repeated collapse of upper airways during sleep, leading to apnea, hypopnea, and sleep disruption. It's a risk factor for cardiovascular diseases (CVDs), hypertension, coronary and cerebrovascular diseases, and heart failure (HF). Observational studies indicate an increased risk of incident hypertension associated with obstructive sleep apnea.
content.CPAP: Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) is effective for improving sleep apnea but its effects on blood pressure reduction are not well established.
content.Nonpharmacological Interventions: Dietary changes, physical activity, and alcohol reduction are crucial for hypertension prevention and management. Behavioral and lifestyle changes are emphasized for prevention.

content.Title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.Synopsis: Nonpharmacological interventions are effective in lowering blood pressure (BP). Key interventions include weight loss, DASH diet, sodium reduction, potassium supplementation, increased physical activity, and moderation of alcohol intake. Other less proven measures include probiotics, increased protein, fiber, flaxseed, fish oil, calcium, magnesium, and other dietary patterns. Stress reduction and certain food items like garlic, dark chocolate, and coffee are less evidential. Behavioral therapies show weak evidence in BP management.

Recommendations.1: Weight loss is recommended for adults with elevated BP or hypertension who are overweight or obese.
Recommendations.2: A heart-healthy diet like DASH is recommended to help achieve weight loss.
Recommendations.3: Sodium reduction is advised for adults with elevated BP or hypertension.
Recommendations.4: Potassium supplementation through diet is recommended unless contraindicated.
Recommendations.6: Alcohol consumption should be limited for adults with elevated BP or hypertension.

content.TableTitle: Best Proven Nonpharmacological Interventions for Prevention and Treatment of Hypertension

Interventions.Name: Weight loss
Interventions.Description: Best goal is ideal body weight, but aim for at least a 1-kg reduction in body weight for most adults who are overweight. Expect about 1 mm Hg for every 1-kg reduction in body weight.
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~5 mm Hg, Normotension: ~2/3 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Healthy diet
Interventions.Description: Consume a diet rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy products, with reduced content of saturated and total fat. This is known as the DASH dietary pattern.
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~11 mm Hg, Normotension: ~3 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Reduced intake of dietary sodium
Interventions.Description: Optimal goal is <1500 mg/d, but aim for at least a 1000-mg/d reduction in most adults.
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~5/6 mm Hg, Normotension: ~2/3 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Enhanced intake of dietary potassium
Interventions.Description: Aim for 3500-5000 mg/d, preferably by consumption of a diet rich in potassium.
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~4/5 mm Hg, Normotension: ~2 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Physical activity
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~5/8 mm Hg, Normotension: ~2/4 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Dynamic resistance
Interventions.Description: 90–150 min/wk, 50%–80% 1 rep maximum, 6 exercises, 3 sets/exercise, 10 repetitions/set
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~4 mm Hg, Normotension: ~2 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Isometric resistance
Interventions.Description: 4 x 2 min (hand grip), 1 min rest between exercises, 30%–40% maximum voluntary contraction, 3 sessions/wk, 8–10 wk
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~5 mm Hg, Normotension: ~4 mm Hg

Interventions.Name: Moderation in alcohol intake
Interventions.Description: In individuals who drink alcohol, reduce alcohol to: Men: ≤2 drinks daily, Women: ≤1 drink daily.
Interventions.Impact: Hypertension: ~4 mm Hg, Normotension: ~3 mm Hg

content.Additional Details: These interventions are fundamental in managing patients with stage 1 and stage 2 hypertension. Weight loss and dietary changes, particularly adopting a DASH or Mediterranean diet, are emphasized as effective measures.

dietary_interventions: The DASH diet has been successful in trials and includes diets low in calories from carbohydrates, high-protein diets, vegetarian diets, and a Mediterranean dietary pattern, all shown to lower BP.
Sodium reduction interventions can prevent hypertension and lower BP, especially in those with higher levels of BP, blacks, older persons.
Dietary potassium is inversely related to BP and hypertension, being effective in lowering BP especially in adults consuming an excess of sodium.
sodium_intake_reduction: Sodium intake reduction by about 25% can result in a 2-3 mm Hg reduction in SBP in nonhypertensive individuals.
Combination with weight loss can substantially double the BP reduction effect.
Reduced dietary sodium is beneficial and can amplify the BP-lowering effects of medication for those with hypertension.
potassium_intake: Higher intake of potassium and a low sodium-high potassium diet is beneficial in reducing hypertension risks.
Potassium supplementation in the form of potassium chloride has been effective in lowering BP.
dietary_recommendations: The 2015 Dietary Guidelines for Americans encourage a diet rich in potassium and identify the adequate intake level as 4700 mg/day.
The World Health Organization recommends a potassium intake of at least 90 mmol (3510 mg) per day.

content.Patient Evaluation: The patient evaluation is meant to identify target organ damage and possible secondary causes of hypertension, assisting in planning an effective treatment regimen. Historical features are crucial in the evaluation, and changes over time may differentiate primary from secondary hypertension. The evaluation includes lifestyle factors, dietary habits, alcohol consumption, tobacco use, and socioeconomic factors. Key diagnostic features and recommended therapies are discussed, particularly lifestyle modifications and the risk factors they address.
content.Physical Activity: Increased physical activity has been shown to lower blood pressure effectively. Different types of exercise, including aerobic, dynamic resistance, and isometric, have demonstrated reductions in systolic BP.
content.Alcohol Consumption: A direct relationship exists between alcohol consumption and blood pressure. Consumption of more than 3 drinks per day increases BP, whereas reducing intake can lower BP. Effects on BP reductions with alcohol abstinence or reduced intake are detailed, especially regarding distinctions in effects based on race.
content.Biomarkers: Alcohol use may influence cardiovascular disease risk markers, such as high-density lipoprotein cholesterol.
content.Risk Factors for Primary Hypertension: Includes gradual increase in BP, lifestyle factors such as weight gain and diet, and family history.
content.Secondary Hypertension Causes: Includes BP lability, obesity, chronic kidney disease, apnea, adrenal issues, and absence of family history.

content.General Information: The document outlines guidelines for managing hypertension, including the importance of accurate blood pressure measurement and laboratory testing. Suggested basic tests include fasting blood glucose, complete blood count, lipid profile, and serum creatinine with eGFR. Optional tests include echocardiogram and uric acid evaluation. The guidelines discuss the significance of left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) and its association with cardiovascular diseases (CVD). LVH is a secondary condition of hypertension, independently predicting further CVD events, and can be assessed via electrocardiography, echocardiography, or MRI. Management strategies such as the use of diuretics and other medications are touched upon.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses the treatment of high blood pressure (BP) with a focus on overall patient health and reducing cardiovascular disease (CVD) outcomes. It emphasizes the use of BP-lowering medications for the prevention of recurrent CVD events in patients with clinical CVD and outlines a comprehensive approach including both nonpharmacological and pharmacological strategies.
Key Recommendations: Use of BP-lowering medications is recommended for secondary prevention of recurrent CVD events in patients with clinical CVD and specific BP thresholds.
BP-lowering medications are recommended for primary prevention in adults with certain ASCVD risk and BP levels.
BP treatment should be guided by an assessment of overall CVD risk.
content.Pharmacological Treatment: The effectiveness of BP-lowering medications in reducing CVD risk is acknowledged across diverse patient groups.
content.BP Management: As BP and future CVD event risks increase, BP management should be intensified.

content.guideline: The clinical guideline focuses on the assessment of cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk and the use of blood pressure (BP) lowering medications. It discusses the importance of using the absolute risk of CVD instead of a specific BP level to guide treatment decisions. The document mentions challenges in implementing CVD risk assessments and suggests the need for international standards. It highlights the use of several CVD risk calculators to determine treatment thresholds and touches on the outcomes of trials like SPRINT.
content.CVD risk assessment: Emphasizes using the ASCVD Risk Estimator to assess 10-year risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) and suggests a BP threshold for treatment in patients with high CVD risk.
content.treatment thresholds: In patients over 79 years of age, the recommended BP threshold for treatment is >130 mm Hg, with suggestions for using predicted CVD risk along with BP to guide treatment decisions.
content.SPRINT trial: Provides evidence for the use of BP-lowering medications in high-risk patients, showing reduced CVD events. Participants with untreated SBP between 130 mm Hg and 139 mm Hg showed benefit with treatment.
content.recommendations: Antihypertensive drug treatment based on CVD risk rather than diabetes or CKD, specifically for older adults with specified BP thresholds. General population and diabetic guidelines are also presented.
content.meta-analysis findings: Supports the BP lowering in high-risk individuals reduces CVD events regardless of initial BP levels.

content.Title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.Figure Overview: Blood Pressure (BP) Thresholds and Recommendations for Treatment and Follow-Up

Recommendations.Normal BP: Promote optimal lifestyle habits. Reassess in 1 y (Class IIa)
Recommendations.Elevated BP: Nonpharmacological therapy (Class I). Reassess in 3–6 mo (Class I)

Stage 1 Hypertension.Low CVD Risk (<10%): Nonpharmacological therapy (Class I). Reassess in 3–6 mo (Class I)
Stage 1 Hypertension.High CVD Risk (\u226510%): Nonpharmacological therapy and BP-lowering medication (Class I). Reassess in 1 mo (Class I)

Recommendations.Stage 2 Hypertension: Nonpharmacological therapy and BP-lowering medication (Class I). Reassess in 1 mo (Class I)

content.Additional Notes: The ACC/AHA Pooled Cohort Equations and the inclusion of DM or CKD in risk assessment. Monitoring post-initiation of RAS inhibitor or diuretic therapy. Consideration of therapy intensification and adherence assessment.

content.risk_of_CVD: Indirect support is provided by evidence from trials using BP-lowering medications to reduce the risk of developing higher levels of BP and to achieve a reduction in left ventricular mass.
content.trial_evidence: In the HOPE-3 Trial, there was no evidence of short-term benefit during treatment of adults (average age 66 years) with a relatively low risk of CVD (3.8% CVD event rate during 5.6 years of follow-up).
content.subgroup_analysis: Suggested benefit in those with an average SBP approximately >140 mm Hg (and a CVD risk of 6.5% during the 5.6 years of follow-up).
content.white_coat_hypertension: Importance of excluding white coat hypertension before initiating pharmacological therapy in patients with low ASCVD risk.

follow_up_recommendations.recommendation: Adults with an elevated BP or stage 1 hypertension with estimated 10-year ASCVD risk <10% should be managed with nonpharmacological therapy and have a repeat BP evaluation within 3 to 6 months.
Adults with stage 1 hypertension with estimated 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% should have a combination of nonpharmacological and antihypertensive drug therapy with a repeat BP evaluation in 1 month.

Adults with stage 2 hypertension should be evaluated by a primary care provider within 1 month, have a combination of nonpharmacological and antihypertensive drug therapy, with repeat BP evaluation in 1 month.
For adults with very high average BP (e.g., SBP ≥180 mm Hg or DBP ≥110 mm Hg), evaluation followed by prompt antihypertensive drug treatment is recommended.
For adults with a normal BP, repeat evaluation every year is reasonable.

content.synopsis: Follow-up is essential in hypertensive patients, with different time frames recommended based on hypertension stage, target organ damage presence, and BP control level.
supportive_text: Nonpharmacological therapy is recommended for adults with elevated BP and first-line for stage 1 hypertension with <10% 10-year ASCVD risk. Adherence to this therapy should be assessed within 3 to 6 months.
Nonpharmacological therapy helps reduce BP in stage 1 hypertension with ≥10% ASCVD risk and should be used as first-line with pharmacotherapy.
Prompt evaluation and treatment in stage 2 hypertension with both drug and nonpharmacological therapy are important due to elevated CVD risk.
Management of very high BP is crucial to reduce target organ damage risk. Treatment should start within at least 1 week depending on the patient's clinical situation.

content.Guideline for Hypertension Management: This clinical guideline provides recommendations for the treatment of high blood pressure (BP) in adults. It emphasizes the use of pharmacological agents, alongside lifestyle modifications, as the primary treatment method. A significant point is that simultaneous use of an ACE inhibitor, ARB, and/or renin inhibitor is potentially harmful and not recommended for treating adults with hypertension. Drug combinations should avoid agents with similar mechanisms of action to prevent harmful effects.
Treatment Recommendations: Use ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and CCBs as primary agents for treatment.
Thiazide diuretics and others may be used based on patient-specific factors like cost, adherence, and comorbidities.
Triple-dose combinations may be less effective if they contain lower-than-optimal doses of thiazide diuretics.
Combination therapy is advised for better BP control and adherence.
Simultaneous administration of some drug combinations can increase cardiovascular risk.

Thiazide or thiazide-type diuretics.Comments: Preferred for its prolonged half-life and proven trial reduction of CVD.
Monitor for hyponatremia, hypokalemia, uric acid, and calcium levels.
Use with caution in patients with history of acute gout unless patient is on uric acid–lowering therapy.

ACE inhibitors.Comments: Do not use in combination with ARBs or direct renin inhibitor.
Increased risk of hyperkalemia, especially in CKD or those on K supplements or K-sparing drugs.
Risk of acute renal failure in severe bilateral renal artery stenosis.
Do not use if history of angioedema with ACE inhibitors.

ARBs.Comments: Do not use in combination with ACE inhibitors or direct renin inhibitor.
Risk of hyperkalemia in CKD or on K supplements or K-sparing drugs.
Risk of acute renal failure in severe bilateral renal artery stenosis.
Do not use if history of angioedema with ARBs.
Patients with a history of angioedema with ACE inhibitor can receive ARB 6 weeks after stopping ACE inhibitor.

CCB\u2013dihydropyridines.Comments: Avoid in patients with HFrEF; amlodipine or felodipine may be used if required.
Associated with dose-related pedal edema, more common in women.

CCB\u2013nondihydropyridines.Comments: Avoid with beta blockers due to increased risk of bradycardia and heart block.
Do not use in patients with HFrEF.
Drug interactions with diltiazem and verapamil (CYP3A4 major substrate and moderate inhibitor).

Diuretics\u2013loop.Comments: Preferred for patients with symptomatic HF, preferred over thiazides in moderate-to-severe CKD (e.g., GFR <30 mL/min).

Diuretics\u2013potassium sparing.Comments: Monotherapy agents, minimally effective antihypertensive agents.

Combination therapy of potassium-sparing diuretic with a thiazide can be considered in patients with hypokalemia on thiazide monotherapy. Avoid in patients with significant CKD (e.g., GFR <45 mL/min).

content.Document Source: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline, JACC VOL. 71, NO. 19, 2018

Comments: Preferred agents in primary aldosteronism and resistant hypertension.
Requires twice-daily dosing for adequate BP lowering.
Associated with greater risk of gynecomastia and impotence compared to eplerenone.
Common add-on therapy in resistant hypertension.
Avoid with K+ supplements, other K+-sparing diuretics, or significant renal dysfunction.
Not recommended as first-line agents unless the patient has IHD or HF.
Preferred in patients with bronchospastic airway disease requiring a beta blocker.
Preferred in patients with HFrEF.
Includes nitric oxide-induced vasodilation.
Avoid in patients with reactive airways disease.
Avoid abrupt cessation.
Generally avoid, especially in patients with IHD or HF.
Preferred in patients with HFrEF.
Do not use in combination with ACE inhibitors or ARBs.
Very long acting.
Increased risk of hyperkalemia in CKD or on K+ supplements.
May cause acute renal failure in severe bilateral renal artery stenosis.
Avoid in pregnancy.
Associated with orthostatic hypotension, especially in older adults.

Drug Classes.Class: Central alpha2-agonist and other centrally acting drugs

Comments: Generally reserved as last-line because of adverse effects, especially in older adults.
Avoid abrupt discontinuation, which may induce hypertensive crisis; taper to avoid rebound hypertension.
Associated with sodium and water retention and reflex tachycardia; use with a diuretic and beta blocker.
Associated with drug-induced lupus-like syndrome at higher doses.
Associated with hirsutism and requires a loop diuretic.
Associated with pericardial effusion.

content.Recommendation: For adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or 10-year ASCVD event risk of 10% or higher, a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg is recommended. For adults with confirmed hypertension, without additional markers of increased CVD risk, a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg may be reasonable.
Study Findings: Intensive BP control improves major CVD outcomes.
Meta-analyses support the benefit of lower BP targets.
Trials like SPRINT and ACCORD tested various BP targets.
More intensive treatment led to significant reduction in primary outcomes for some trials.
SPRINT showed significant reduction in CVD events with intensive BP target.
Specific Recommendations: Meta-analysis indicates intensive BP reduction significantly reduces risk of stroke, coronary events, and cardiovascular mortality.
An additional 10-mm Hg reduction in SBP significantly reduces CVD risk.

content.Title: Whelton et al. 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.Context: The guideline discusses the treatment of patients with hypertension, emphasizing the choice of initial medication and the reduction of cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk.

Recommendations.Recommendation: For initiation of antihypertensive drug therapy, first-line agents include thiazide diuretics, CCBs, and ACE inhibitors or ARBs.

content.Summary: Most patients with sufficiently elevated blood pressure (BP) should be treated initially with two agents. Selection of agents should consider conditions such as heart failure (HF) or chronic kidney disease (CKD). In trials, thiazide-type diuretics and CCBs have been effective in lowering BP and preventing stroke, particularly in black patients. Beta blockers may be less effective for stroke prevention but can be useful when combined with other agents.

The guideline recommends initiating antihypertensive drug therapy with 2 first-line agents for adults with stage 2 hypertension and an average BP more than 20/10 mm Hg above their target. For adults with stage 1 hypertension and a BP goal <130/80 mm Hg, initiating treatment with a single antihypertensive drug is reasonable. Combination therapy is favorable for higher BP control, especially in those with high CVD risk or a history of hypotension. Recommendations also emphasize tailoring drug therapy to individual traits.
Recommendations: Initiate antihypertensive therapy with 2 first-line agents for patients with stage 2 hypertension.
Single-agent therapy is reasonable for stage 1 hypertension with a BP goal <130/80 mm Hg.
Considerations: Combination therapy is often required for effective BP control.
Caution advised when initiating 2 drugs in older adults due to potential hypotension.
Therapy should be tailored to individual patient characteristics and comorbidities.

content.Initial Drug and Monitoring: For patients where the initial drug is not tolerated or effective, changes may be needed, involving alternate medications and BP monitoring. Approximately 25% of patients will need further treatment adjustments. Goal BP can be challenging in some patients.
content.Options and Considerations: Diuretic therapy should be adjusted based on conditions like hypokalemia, secondary aldosteronism, or specific intolerances like cough from ACE inhibitors. Alternatives include ARBs or beta-1 selective blockers for certain conditions.
content.Lifestyle Recommendations: Patients with low CVD risk should consider lifestyle modifications along with drug therapy, emphasizing BP monitoring and adherence to improvements.
content.Follow-up and Reassessment: Regular follow-up and assessments of adherence, response, and therapy adjustments are essential, particularly for patients starting or changing antihypertensive drug therapy.

Recommendations.Text: Follow-up and monitoring after initiation of drug therapy for hypertension control should include systematic strategies to help improve BP, including use of HBPM, team-based care, and telehealth strategies.

Supportive Text.Text: Systematic approaches to follow-up have been shown to improve hypertension control and can be adapted and incorporated into clinical practices according to local needs and resource availability.

Certain comorbidities may affect clinical decision-making in hypertension. These include ischemic heart disease, HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), HFpEF, CKD, cerebrovascular disease, AF, PAD, DM, and metabolic syndrome.
The guideline recommends use of BP-lowering medications for secondary prevention of CVD in patients with clinical CVD (CHD, HF, and stroke) and an average BP ≥130/80 mm Hg.

content.title: Recommendations for Treatment of Hypertension in Patients With Stable Ischemic Heart Disease (SIHD)

recommendations.summary: In adults with SIHD and hypertension, a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg is recommended.

Adults with SIHD and hypertension (BP ≥130/80 mm Hg) should be treated with medications (e.g., GDMT beta blockers, ACE inhibitors, or ARBs) for compelling indications (e.g., previous MI, stable angina) as first-line therapy, with addition of other drugs (dihydropyridine CCBs, thiazide diuretics, and/or mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists) as needed.
In adults with SIHD with angina and persistent uncontrolled hypertension, the addition of dihydropyridine CCBs to GDMT (including beta blockers) is recommended.
In adults who have had an MI or acute coronary syndrome, it is reasonable to continue GDMT beta blockers beyond 3 years as long-term therapy for hypertension.
Beta blockers and/or CCBs might be considered to control hypertension in patients with CAD (without HFrEF) who had an MI more than 3 years ago and have angina.

content.synopsis: Hypertension is a major risk factor for ischemic heart disease. Randomized trials have shown that antihypertensive therapy reduces risk of ischemic heart disease and cardiovascular events. Reduction of SBP to <130/80 mm Hg reduces CVD complications and all-cause mortality. Specific agents such as ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril, perindopril), ARBs, beta blockers, dihydropyridine CCBs, thiazide diuretics, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists are discussed for use in SIHD.
content.figure_summary: Flowchart: For patients with Hypertension and SIHD, reduce BP to <130/80 mm Hg with GDMT (beta blockers, ACE inhibitor, or ARB). If BP goal not met and angina present, add dihydropyridine CCBs; if no angina, add dihydropyridine CCBs, thiazide-type diuretics, and/or MRAs as needed.

genes.context: Text repeatedly references "ACE inhibitors" and expands ACE as "angiotensin-converting enzyme" as a recommended therapy for hypertension in patients with SIHD and for secondary prevention after MI.
The guideline refers to "ARBs" (angiotensin receptor blockers) as a class of agents recommended for treating hypertension in SIHD; ARBs act on the angiotensin II receptor (commonly AGTR1).
The recommendations list "mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists" (MRAs) as an added therapy option for further BP control; the mineralocorticoid receptor is encoded by NR3C2.

content.clinical_guideline: The guideline discusses treatments for hypertension in patients with Stable Ischemic Heart Disease (SIHD) and heart failure. Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) beta blockers such as carvedilol, metoprolol tartrate, metoprolol succinate, nadolol, bisoprolol, propranolol, and timolol are recommended. Calcium channel blockers (CCBs), like dihydropyridine, are also effective against hypertension and angina.

recommendations.recommendation: In adults at increased risk of heart failure (HF), the optimal blood pressure in those with hypertension should be less than 130/80 mm Hg.

studies.cardiovascular_health_study: The incidence of heart failure is significantly higher in individuals with antecedent hypertension.
studies.health_aging_body_composition_study: Indicates increased incidence of heart failure at higher systolic blood pressures.
studies.SPRINT: More intensive treatment targeting SBP <120 mm Hg reduced cardiovascular disease composite outcomes and heart failure.

content.title: 9.2.1 Heart Failure With Reduced Ejection Fraction

recommendations.recommendation: Adults with HFrEF and hypertension should be prescribed GDMT titrated to attain a BP of less than 130/80 mm Hg.
Nonhydropyridine CCBs are not recommended in the treatment of hypertension in adults with HFrEF.

content.synopsis: Approximately 50% of patients with HF have HFrEF. Treatment with GDMT reduces mortality and hospitalizations. Antihypertensive therapy reduces the incidence of HF in hypertensive patients. In the ALLHAT trial, chlorthalidone reduced the risk of HFrEF more than amlodipine and doxazosin but similarly to lisinopril.

recommendation_specific_support.point: Based on 2017 ACC/AHA/HFSA guideline focusing on heart failure.
recommendation_specific_support.lifestyle_modifications: Weight loss and sodium reduction may help. No RTC evidence for superiority of one medication over another.
recommendation_specific_support.medications: Effective first-line include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, receptor-neprilysin inhibitors, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists, diuretics, and GDMT beta blockers.

recommendation_specific_support.point: Nonhydropyridine CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem) have myocardial depressant activity and are not recommended for those with HFrEF.

content.Synopsis: Approximately 50% of patients with HF have HFpEF. The ejection fraction in these studies has varied from >40% to <55%. Patients with HFpEF are usually older women with a history of hypertension. Obesity, CHD, DM, AF, and hyperlipidemia are also highly prevalent in patients with HFpEF. Hypertension is the most important cause of HFpEF, with a prevalence of 60% to 89% in large RCTs, epidemiological studies, and HF registries. Patients with HFpEF also have an exaggerated hypertensive response to exercise. Hypertensive acute pulmonary edema is an expression of HFpEF.
content.Blood Pressure Control: BP control is important for prevention of HFpEF in patients with hypertension (ALLHAT study). Treatment with chlorthalidone reduced the risk of HF compared with amlodipine, doxazosin, and lisinopril. Improved BP control also reduces hospitalization, CVD events, and mortality.
content.Treatment: Diuretics are the only drugs used for the treatment of hypertension and HF that can adequately control the fluid retention of HF. Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibition with ACE inhibitor or ARB and especially MRA would represent the preferred choice.
Recommendations: Patients with hypertension and CKD should be treated to a BP goal of less than 130/80 mm Hg.
In adults with hypertension and CKD (stage 3 or higher or stage 1 or 2 with albuminuria), treatment with an ACE inhibitor is reasonable to slow kidney disease progression.
In adults with hypertension and CKD (stage 3 or higher or stage 1 or 2 with albuminuria), treatment with an ARB may be reasonable if an ACE inhibitor is not tolerated.

content.title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.synopsis: The guideline emphasizes the systematic review for managing high blood pressure in adults, focusing on the correlation between hypertension and chronic kidney disease (CKD). Hypertension is prevalent in 67% to 92% of patients with CKD. Systematic blood pressure (BP) management is crucial to prevent further kidney function decline. A tighter BP target for CKD patients is optimal, with specific recommendations based on the SPRINT trial results. Treatment may include ACE inhibitors or ARBs, with a BP goal of <130/80 mm Hg for patients with CKD, particularly those with albuminuria.
content.hypertension: It is the most common comorbidity affecting patients with CKD, linked with increased prevalence as kidney function declines.
content.BP target: Recommended BP target is <130/80 mm Hg for CKD patients based on SPRINT trial results.
content.treatment recommendations: ACE inhibitors are recommended, particularly for those with albuminuria. If intolerant, ARBs are suggested as an alternative.

content.Guideline: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline

Recommendations.Recommendation: After kidney transplantation, it is reasonable to treat patients with hypertension to a BP goal of less than 130/80 mm Hg.
After kidney transplantation, it is reasonable to treat patients with hypertension with a calcium antagonist on the basis of improved GFR and kidney survival.

Hypertension After Renal Transplantation.Use of calcineurin inhibitor: Associated with high prevalence of hypertension.

CKD Management: Recommendation to assess ASCVD risk in adults with hypertension including CKD.
Greatest benefit with intensive BP treatment observed in patients >75 years.
ASCVD risk ≥10% identified for high risk antihypertensive therapy.
Research and Evidence: Evidence supports intensive BP treatment in patients with CKD, but lacks proof for kidney disease progression reduction.
ARBs have similar efficacy to ACE inhibitors.

content.clinical_guideline: Management of Hypertension in Patients With Acute Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH) recommends lowering systolic blood pressure (SBP) to <140 mm Hg for SBP levels between 150-220 mm Hg (Class III: Harm), and continuous IV infusion with close BP monitoring for SBP levels >220 mm Hg (Class IIa). Evidence indicates intensive BP reduction improves functional recovery at 3 months. Immediate BP lowering <140/90 mm Hg within 6 hours is feasible but did not significantly reduce death or severe disability at 3 months, with some trials showing increased renal adverse events.

recommendations.description: Adults with acute ischemic stroke and elevated BP who are eligible for treatment with intravenous tissue plasminogen activator should have their BP slowly lowered to less than 185/110 mm Hg before thrombolytic therapy is initiated.

In adults with an acute ischemic stroke, BP should be less than 185/110 mm Hg before administration of intravenous tissue plasminogen activator and should be maintained below 180/105 mm Hg for at least the first 24 hours after initiating drug therapy.

Starting or restarting antihypertensive therapy during hospitalization in patients with BP greater than 140/90 mm Hg who are neurologically stable is safe and reasonable to improve long-term BP control, unless contraindicated.

In patients with BP of 220/120 mm Hg or higher who did not receive intravenous alteplase or endovascular treatment and have no comorbid conditions requiring acute antihypertensive treatment, the benefit of initiating or reinitiating treatment of hypertension within the first 48 to 72 hours is uncertain. It might be reasonable to lower BP by 15% during the first 24 hours after onset of stroke.

In patients with BP less than 220/120 mm Hg who did not receive intravenous thrombolysis or endovascular treatment and do not have a comorbid condition requiring acute antihypertensive treatment, initiating or reinitiating treatment of hypertension within the first 48 to 72 hours after an acute ischemic stroke is not effective to prevent death or dependency.

content.synopsis: Elevated BP is common during acute ischemic stroke (occurring in up to 80% of patients), especially among patients with a history of hypertension. However, BP often decreases spontaneously during this phase. The recommendations focus on managing BP in specific situations, especially when using intravenous tissue plasminogen activator.

content.Figure Information: Algorithm on management of hypertension in patients with acute ischemic stroke.
content.Initial Assessment: Patients with acute (<72 h from symptom onset) ischemic stroke and elevated BP.
content.IV Thrombolysis Treatment: For patients qualifying for IV thrombolysis, lower SBP to <185 mm Hg and DBP <110 mm Hg before initiation, and maintain BP <180/105 mm Hg for 24 h after IV thrombolysis (Class I).
content.BP Management in Non-thrombolysis: For BP >220/110 mm Hg, lower BP 15% during the first 24 h (Class Iib).
content.Non-treatment Under Certain Conditions: BP <220/110 mm Hg, initiating or reinitiating treatment within 48-72 hours is ineffective to prevent death or dependency. (Class III: No Benefit).
content.Reinitiate Treatment: For preexisting hypertension, reinitiate antihypertensive drugs after neurological stability (Class IIa).
Supportive Text: BP cutoffs aligned with clinical trials for intravenous thrombolysis.
High BP during initial 24 hours linked to higher risk of symptomatic ICH.
Extreme arterial hypertension leads to encephalopathy, cardiac compromise, renal damage.
Rapid hypotension can be harmful due to reduced organ perfusion.
RCT data indicates antihypertensive agents reduce BP but not beneficial for mortality or dependency during acute phase.

Recommendations.Description: Adults with previously treated hypertension who experience a stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA) should be restarted on antihypertensive treatment after the first few days of the index event to reduce the risk of recurrent stroke and other vascular events.
For adults who experience a stroke or TIA, treatment with a thiazide diuretic, ACE inhibitor, or ARB, or combination treatment consisting of a thiazide diuretic plus ACE inhibitor, is useful.

Adults not previously treated for hypertension who experience a stroke or TIA and have an established BP of 140/90 mm Hg or higher should be prescribed antihypertensive treatment a few days after the index event to reduce the risk of recurrent stroke and other vascular events.
For adults who experience a stroke or TIA, selection of specific drugs should be individualized on the basis of patient comorbidities and agent pharmacological class.
For adults who experience a stroke or TIA, a BP goal of less than 130/80 mm Hg may be reasonable.
For adults with a lacunar stroke, a target SBP goal of less than 130 mm Hg may be reasonable.

In adults previously untreated for hypertension who experience an ischemic stroke or TIA and have a SBP less than 140 mm Hg and a DBP less than 90 mm Hg, the usefulness of initiating antihypertensive treatment is not well established.

content.Synopsis: Each year in the United States, over 750,000 adult patients experience a stroke, with up to 25% being recurrent strokes. For an individual who experiences an initial stroke or TIA, the annual risk of a subsequent stroke is 4%, with a case mortality rate of 41% after another stroke. Between 70% of patients with a recent stroke or TIA have prevailing hypertension.

content.title: Management of Hypertension in Patients With a Previous History of Stroke (Secondary Stroke Prevention)
general_guideline: For patients with a prior stroke or TIA, antihypertensive treatment is recommended if hypertension is present.
Class I recommendation to restart or initiate antihypertensive treatment if SBP ≥140 mm Hg or DBP ≥90 mm Hg.
Class IIb recommendation if SBP <140 mm Hg and DBP <90 mm Hg, where treatment usefulness is not well established.
evidence: RCT and meta-analysis data supports treatment in patients with stroke or TIA history.
Greater reductions in SBP are associated with reduced stroke risk.
Diuretics, ACE inhibitors, and ARBs are beneficial.
SBP levels <130 mm Hg not clearly linked to lower stroke risk.
key_points: Reduction in BP is prioritized over specific agent choice.
Lacunar stroke patients targeting <130 mm Hg may have reduced ICH risk.
Post hoc analysis suggests diminished effectiveness as BP declines from higher baselines.

9.5 Peripheral Artery Disease.Recommendation for Treatment: Adults with hypertension and PAD should be treated similarly to patients with hypertension without PAD.
9.5 Peripheral Artery Disease.Synopsis: Patients with PAD are at increased risk of CVD and stroke, where hypertension is a major risk factor for PAD.
9.5 Peripheral Artery Disease.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: No major difference in relative risk reduction in CVD from BP-lowering therapy between patients with hypertension and PAD and patients without PAD.

Recommendations: Antihypertensive drug treatment should be initiated at a BP of 130/80 mm Hg or higher for patients with DM and hypertension.
All first-line classes of antihypertensive agents are useful and effective.
ACE inhibitors or ARBs may be considered in the presence of albuminuria.
9.6 Diabetes Mellitus.Synopsis: Prevalence of hypertension among adults with DM is approximately 80%. Hypertension in these individuals significantly increases the risk of CVD damage, stroke, and mortality.

main_points: The guideline discusses blood pressure targets for adults with diabetes mellitus (DM) and hypertension.
There is limited RCT evidence supporting specific BP targets (<140 mm Hg vs. <120 mm Hg) for adults with DM.
Several trials, including ACCORD and SPRINT, are referenced, but findings are mixed.
The recommendation emphasizes ASCVD risk assessment in all adults with hypertension and DM.
Various antihypertensive drug classes are mentioned as useful, particularly ACE inhibitors and ARBs for patients with DM.
Metabolic syndrome is discussed as a condition related to DM, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease risk.

clinical_information: Metabolic syndrome is linked to various disorders including CKD, nonalcoholic steatohepatitis, polycystic ovary syndrome, certain cancers, Alzheimer's disease, Cushing's syndrome, lipodystrophy, and hyperalimentation.
The optimal antihypertensive therapy for patients with hypertension in the setting of the metabolic syndrome has not been clearly defined.
Caution is advised with thiazide diuretics as they may increase insulin resistance and accelerate conversion to overt DM.
Chlorthalidone use was associated with a small increase in fasting glucose levels.
ARB therapy may reduce arterial stiffness in patients with hypertension and the metabolic syndrome.
Lifestyle modification is recommended to improve insulin sensitivity.
AF and hypertension are common coexistent conditions, with AF more prevalent in older populations.
Treatment of hypertension may prevent new-onset AF, especially in patients with LVH or LV dysfunction.
Use of traditional beta blockers may lead to dyslipidemia and deterioration of glucose tolerance.
Vasodilating beta blockers show neutral or favorable effects on metabolic profiles.
Anticoagulation control of hypertension is key in patients with AF.

content.Hypertension: Hypertension is a risk factor for the development of aortic stenosis. In patients with symptomatic or asymptomatic aortic valve disease, hypertension should be treated cautiously. RAS blockade is recommended in patients with prior AF relative to other classes of agents.

Valvular Heart Disease Recommendations.Recommendation: In adults with asymptomatic aortic stenosis, hypertension should be treated with pharmacotherapy, starting at a low dose and gradually titrating upward as needed.
In patients with chronic aortic insufficiency, treatment of systolic hypertension with agents that do not slow the heart rate is reasonable.

content.Beta Blockers: May be appropriate for patients with aortic stenosis who have reduced ejection fraction, prior MI, arrhythmias, or angina pectoris.

content.Topic: Management of Hypertension in Patients with Aortic Disease
content.Recommendation: Beta blockers are recommended as the preferred antihypertensive agents in patients with hypertension and thoracic aortic disease.
content.Synopsis: Thoracic aortic aneurysms are typically asymptomatic until a catastrophic event such as aortic dissection or rupture occurs. Antihypertensive therapy is largely based on studies associating hypertension with aortic dissection. High BP variability was an independent risk factor for the prognosis of aortic dissection.
content.Ethnic and Racial Considerations: Hypertension control rates are lower for blacks, Hispanic Americans, and Asian Americans than for whites. Black Americans face significantly greater risks of hypertension-related complications and mortality compared to whites.
content.Specific Genetic Note: Some black individuals with hypertension may have the APOL1 gene variant, which is associated with a higher risk of chronic kidney disease and may affect response to certain BP lowering medications.

genes.context: Finally, the excess risk of CKD outcomes in at least some blacks with hypertension may be due to the presence of high-risk APOL1 (apolipoprotein L1) genetic variants.

content.synopsis: Lifestyle modification (e.g., weight reduction, dietary modification, increased physical activity) is important in black and Hispanic Americans for hypertension prevention and therapy. The adoption of these recommendations is challenging due to socioeconomic barriers. Principles of antihypertensive drug selection should apply with considerations for ethnic groups. Thiazide-type diuretics and CCBs are effective in lowering BP among blacks. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are recommended for black patients with hypertension, DM, and nephropathy. Combination therapy is often necessary for effective BP control in blacks.

recommendations.recommendation: In black adults with hypertension but without HF or CKD, initial antihypertensive treatment should include a thiazide-type diuretic or CCB.
Two or more antihypertensive medications are recommended to achieve a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg in most adults with hypertension, especially in black adults.

key_points: Thiazide-type diuretics or CCBs are more effective in lowering BP in blacks compared to RAS inhibitors or beta blockers.
Four drug classes (thiazide diuretic, CCB, ACE inhibitor, or ARB) lower BP and are recommended.

content.Women and Hypertension: The guideline discusses the effect of intensive Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP) reduction in women. It highlights that there was no significant difference between men and women in cardiovascular disease (CVD) outcomes from a meta-analysis of clinical trials.
content.CVD Trials: There was a large meta-analysis involving 100,000 men and 90,000 women which did not show significant differences in CVD outcomes between sexes.
content.Beta Blockers: Beta blockers associated with reduced mortality in men but not in women.
content.Adverse Drug Effects: Women more frequently experienced ACE inhibitor–induced cough and edema with calcium antagonists.

Pregnancy and Hypertension.Management: Recommendations focus on transitioning pregnant women to methyldopa, nifedipine, or labetalol.
Pregnancy and Hypertension.Severe Hypertension: Treatment reduces risk of severe hypertension by 50% but does not prevent other complications like preeclampsia or preterm birth.
Pregnancy and Hypertension.Contraindicated Drugs: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated during pregnancy.

content.Pregnancy and Hypertension: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are not recommended due to fetotoxicity during pregnancy. Various international guidelines exist for managing hypertension in pregnancy. Magnesium and aspirin may be used in severe cases.
content.Preeclampsia: Preeclampsia occurs in 3.8% of pregnancies and accounts for 9% of maternal deaths in the U.S. It is associated with increased risks like preterm delivery and fetal growth restriction. Screening is recommended at every prenatal visit.
content.Older Persons and Hypertension: For older adults (≥65 years), a SBP treatment goal of less than 130 mm Hg is recommended. Isolated systolic hypertension is the main form. DBP decreases while SBP continues to rise with age.

content.title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.citation: Whelton et al.; JACC Vol. 71, No. 19, May 15, 2018
content.section_focus: BP lowering in older adults; recommendation-specific supportive text; brief note on children and adolescents
content.summary: Discusses benefits and challenges of blood pressure (BP) lowering in older adults, evidence from randomized controlled trials (HYVET, SPRINT), risk thresholds for initiating antihypertensive therapy, orthostatic hypotension concerns, and recommendation to perform ASCVD risk assessment in adults with hypertension. Also notes pediatric guideline sources.
definitions: Isolated systolic hypertension defined in text as SBP ≥160 mm Hg with variable DBP ≤90, ≤95, or ≤110 mm Hg.
key_points: RCTs over past decades show BP lowering reduces risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) and mortality in many older adults, including those ≥65, 75, and 80 years, and can be safe when monitored.
HYVET and SPRINT included independently living older persons and demonstrated benefit; SPRINT showed benefit for an SBP goal of <120 mm Hg but excluded persons with standing SBP <110 mm Hg.
Intensive BP control increases risk of acute kidney injury but overall similar to younger adults; orthostatic hypotension risk requires careful monitoring in older persons.
NHANES (2011–2014) analysis indicates high prevalence of predicted 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10% or prior CVD among older adults (e.g., ~88% of adults ≥65 years; for ≥75 years, 100% met criteria).
For convenience most older adults have a 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%, supporting initiation of antihypertensive therapy at BP ≥130/80 mm Hg for many high-risk older persons (see referenced sections).

Patients with prevalent/frequent falls, advanced cognitive impairment, and multiple comorbidities may be at increased risk of adverse outcomes with intensive BP lowering and may not have been well represented in RCTs.

trials_and_data_sources.name: HYVET
trials_and_data_sources.context: Hypertension in the Very Elderly Trial; included older persons living independently; stopped early for benefit (HYVET after 1.8 years).

trials_and_data_sources.name: SPRINT
trials_and_data_sources.context: Included older ambulatory patients, benefit seen for SBP goal <120 mm Hg; excluded persons with standing SBP <110 mm Hg; follow-up and outcomes discussed.

trials_and_data_sources.name: NHANES (2011–2014)
trials_and_data_sources.context: Used to estimate proportion of older adults with 10-year predicted ASCVD risk ≥10% or prior CVD (reported ~88% of adults ≥65 years; ~98% men and ~80% women ≥65; persons ≥75 years, 100%).

recommendations.text: Recommend ASCVD risk assessment in all adults with hypertension, including older persons; many older adults have 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%, supporting initiation of antihypertensive therapy at BP ≥130/80 mm Hg for high-risk individuals.

Initiation of antihypertensive therapy with two agents should be undertaken cautiously in older persons; monitor carefully for orthostatic hypotension and falls; individualized management for those with frailty, dementia, or advanced comorbidities.

special_considerations: Orthostatic hypotension and neurogenic orthostatic hypotension (e.g., in Parkinson’s disease) are important when treating older persons.
Older persons in nursing homes, those with prevalent dementia, and those with advanced heart failure were largely excluded from large RCTs and require individualized care.
Pediatric guidelines exist from other organizations; 2011 updates and 2004 report referenced for pediatric BP thresholds and stages.
content.page_notes: Text includes multiple internal cross-references (e.g., Sections and Supplemental Items S10.3.1-...); recommendation-supportive text and data summaries appear on this page.

content.Topic: Resistant Hypertension - Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Treatment
content.Guideline Overview: The guideline addresses resistant hypertension, defined as blood pressure that remains above goal despite the use of three antihypertensive medications of different classes, one of which should be a diuretic. It includes steps such as confirming treatment resistance, excluding pseudoresistance, identifying and minimizing contributing lifestyle factors and interfering substances, screening for secondary causes of hypertension, and implementing pharmacological treatment.
Diagnosis Steps: Confirm treatment resistance with office SBP/DBP ≥130/80 mm Hg despite optimal medication or requiring ≥4 medications to achieve control.
Exclude pseudoresistance by ensuring accurate measurements and adherence.
Identify and reverse lifestyle factors like obesity and high-salt intake.
Discontinue or minimize interfering substances such as NSAIDs and stimulants.
Screen for secondary causes like primary aldosteronism and CKD.
Treatment Recommendations: Use different mechanism agents and loop diuretics if needed.
Refer to specialists if blood pressure is uncontrolled after 6 months.

content.Resistant Hypertension: Resistant hypertension is when BP does not achieve control or when BP control is achieved but requires 4 or more medications. The prevalence in the adult population is approximately 13%, increasing to 20% for patients with elevated CHD risk factors. Management includes considering patient characteristics, pseudoresistance, and screening for secondary causes of hypertension.
content.Pharmacological Therapy: Combination of medications with complementary mechanisms of action provides an empirical approach that enhances BP control. Specifically, a 3-drug regimen is advocated including CCBs, RAS inhibitors, and chlorthalidone. Spironolactone addition to this regimen provides substantial BP reduction compared to placebo.
content.Treatment Strategy: Spironolactone shows advantage over other active drugs. The PATHWAY-2 RCT demonstrated its superiority over alpha and beta blockers. Hydralazine or minoxidil may be added if the patient is resistant to the usual combination therapy. Dosing of multidrug regimens might include nighttime dosing.
content.Refractory Hypertension: Refractory hypertension is defined as failure to control BP despite the use of at least 5 agents. It relates to patients with high rates of CVD complications.

content.Synopsis: Hypertensive emergencies are defined as severe elevations in blood pressure (BP) (>180/120 mm Hg) with evidence of new or worsening target organ damage. The 1-year death rate is >79%, and median survival is 10.4 months if untreated. Examples include hypertensive encephalopathy, ICH, acute ischemic stroke, acute left ventricular failure, and pulmonary edema. Immediate reduction of BP is recommended.

Hypertensive Emergency.Criteria: Severe BP elevation with target organ damage.

BP Reduction Strategy.Aortic Dissection: Reduce SBP to <140 mm Hg during the first hour.
BP Reduction Strategy.Others: Reduce BP by max 25% over first hour, then gradually to normal over next 24-48 hours.

Markedly Elevated BP Without Target Orga.Action: Reinstitute/intensify oral antihypertensive drug therapy and arrange follow-up.

content.title: Intravenous Antihypertensive Drugs for Treatment of Hypertensive Emergencies

CCB--dihydropyridines.usual_dose_range: Initial 5 mg/h, increasing every 5 min by 2.5 mg/h to a maximum 15 mg/h.
CCB--dihydropyridines.comments: Contraindicated in advanced aortic stenosis; no dose adjustment needed for elderly.

CCB--dihydropyridines.usual_dose_range: Initial 1–2 mg/h, doubling every 90 s until BP approaches target, then increasing by less than double every 5–10 min; maximum dose 32 mg/h; maximum duration 72 h.
CCB--dihydropyridines.comments: Contraindicated in patients with soybean, soy product, egg allergy and defective lipid metabolism. Use low-end dose range for elderly patients.

Vasodilators--Nitric-oxide dependent.usual_dose_range: Initial 0.3–0.5 mcg/kg/min; increase in increments of 0.5 mcg/kg/min to achieve BP target; maximum dose 10 mcg/kg/min; duration of treatment as short as possible.
Vasodilators--Nitric-oxide dependent.comments: Intra-arterial BP monitoring recommended. Tachyphylaxis common with extended use. Cyanide toxicity with prolonged use.

Vasodilators--Nitric-oxide dependent.usual_dose_range: Initial 5 mcg/min; increase in increments of 5 mcg/min every 3–5 min to a maximum of 20 mcg/min.
Vasodilators--Nitric-oxide dependent.comments: Use only in patients with acute coronary syndrome and/or acute pulmonary edema.

Vasodilators--direct.usual_dose_range: Initial 10 mg via slow IV infusion (maximum initial dose 20 mg); repeat every 4–6 h as needed.
Vasodilators--direct.comments: BP begins to decrease within 10–30 min, and the full lasts 2–6 h. Unpredictability of response.

Adrenergic blockers--beta1 receptor sele.usual_dose_range: Loading dose 500–1000 mcg/kg/min over 1 min followed by a 50–150 mcg/kg/min infusion.
Adrenergic blockers--beta1 receptor sele.comments: Contraindicated in patients with current beta-blocker therapy. Monitor for bradycardia.

Adrenergic blockers--combined alpha and.usual_dose_range: Initial 0.3–1.0 mg/kg dose (maximum 20 mg) slow IV injection or 0.4–1.0 mg/kg/h IV infusion up to 3 mg/kg/h.
Adrenergic blockers--combined alpha and.comments: Contraindicated in reactive airways disease or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Adrenergic blockers--nonselective alpha.usual_dose_range: IV bolus dose 5 mg. Additional bolus doses every 10 min as needed to lower BP to target.
Adrenergic blockers--nonselective alpha.comments: Used in hypertensive emergencies induced by catecholamine excess.

Dopamine-receptor selective agonist.usual_dose_range: Initial 0.1–0.3 mcg/kg/min; may be increased in increments of 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min every 15 min until target BP is reached.
Dopamine-receptor selective agonist.comments: Contraindicated in patients at risk of increased intraocular pressure.

ACE inhibitor.usual_dose_range: Initial 1.25 mg over 5 min. Doses can be increased up to 5 mg every 6 h as needed to achieve BP target.
ACE inhibitor.comments: Contraindicated in pregnancy and should not be used in acute MI or bilateral renal artery stenosis.

notes: There is no RCT evidence that antihypertensive drugs reduce mortality in patients with hypertensive emergencies.
Selection of antihypertensive agent should be based on the drug’s pharmacology and patient’s needs.

content.Title: Intravenous Antihypertensive Drugs for Treatment of Hypertensive Emergencies in Patients With Selected Comorbidities

Acute aortic dissection.Comments: Requires rapid lowering of SBP to ≤120 mm Hg. Beta blockade should precede vasodilator administration to prevent reflex tachycardia or inotropic effect; SBP ≤120 mm Hg should be achieved within 20 min.

Acute coronary syndromes.Comments: Nitrates given in presence of PDE-5 inhibitors may induce profound hypotension. Contraindications to beta blockers include conditions such as moderate-to-severe LV failure with pulmonary edema.

Eclampsia or preeclampsia.Comments: Requires rapid BP lowering. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, renin inhibitors, and nitroprusside contraindicated.

Perioperative hypertension (BP \u2265160.Comments: Intraoperative hypertension is most frequently seen during anesthesia induction and airway manipulation.

Clinical guidelines.Preventing target organ damage: The therapeutic goal is to minimize target organ damage safely by rapid recognition of the problem and early initiation of appropriate antihypertensive treatment.
Clinical guidelines.Compelling conditions for rapid lowering: Includes acute dissection, severe preeclampsia or eclampsia, and pheochromocytoma with hypertensive crisis.
Clinical guidelines.RCT evidence: There is no RCT evidence comparing different strategies to reduce BP except in patients with ICH.
Clinical guidelines.BP reduction caution: Excessive reduction of BP may cause or contribute to renal, cerebral, or coronary ischemia and should be avoided.

Recommendations for Cognitive Decline an.Recommendation: In adults with hypertension, BP lowering is reasonable to prevent cognitive decline and dementia.

content.Synopsis: Dementia is a leading cause of mortality and affects millions globally. The incidence is expected to double by 2050. A 5-year delay in its onset could decrease cases by about 50%. Vascular disease and its risk factors, including hypertension, are implicated in dementia, including Alzheimer's. Better control of systolic blood pressure (SBP) may reduce the risk of Alzheimer's disease and other dementias.

Recommendations.Clinical Trials: Five trials assessed the impact of BP lowering on incident dementia. Four showed a reduction in incidence, two with statistical significance. SYST-EUR and PROGRESS showed reductions in incident dementia with specific SBP targets. No trial shows adverse effects of BP lowering on cognition.
Recommendations.Sexual Dysfunction and Hypertension: There is an association between sexual dysfunction, atherosclerosis, and hypertension. Endothelial dysfunction is common, but evidence on screening for CVD factors in men with erectile dysfunction is insufficient. Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, effective for erectile dysfunction, also have implications for vascular disease.

Preoperative Recommendations: Beta blockers should be continued for patients undergoing major surgery who have been taking them chronically.
For patients undergoing planned elective major surgery, it is reasonable to continue medical therapy for hypertension until surgery.
Discontinuation of ACE inhibitors or ARBs perioperatively may be considered for those undergoing major surgery.
Deferring surgery may be considered for patients with planned elective major surgery and SBP ≥180 mm Hg or DBP ≥110 mm Hg.
Abrupt preoperative discontinuation of beta blockers or clonidine may be harmful for patients undergoing surgery.
Beta blockers should not be started on the day of surgery in beta blocker–naïve patients.
Intraoperative Recommendations: Patients with intraoperative hypertension should be managed with intravenous medications until oral medications can be resumed.

Synopsis.Hypertension in the perioperative period: Strategies recommend assessing contributing factors such as volume status and pain control. Uncontrolled hypertension is associated with complications. Rebound hypertension can occur from abrupt cessation of certain medications.

recommendations.title: Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text
details: Beta blockers should be continued in patients who are currently on them for long-term reasons like myocardial infarction and stable angina.
Control of blood pressure to target levels is recommended before major elective procedures using either oral or intravenous medications.
Potential risk and benefit of ACE inhibitors in the perioperative setting. Stopping ACE inhibitors 24 hours before surgery might reduce adverse outcomes.
Conflicting evidence for patients with DBP ≥110 mm Hg suggests delay of surgery.
Studies describe risks of withdrawing beta blockers perioperatively but abrupt withdrawal is harmful.
Recommendations about starting beta blockers on the day of surgery if patients are beta-blocker-naïve.
Various antihypertensive agents are available for treatment of hypertensive emergencies.

recommendations.title: Strategies to Improve Hypertension Treatment and Control
details: Promoting treatment adherence and individual-based treatment to improve control of hypertension.

content.title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline

recommendations.statement: In adults with hypertension, dosing of antihypertensive medication once daily rather than multiple times daily is beneficial to improve adherence.
Use of combination pills rather than free individual components can be useful to improve adherence to antihypertensive therapy.

content.synopsis: Up to 25% of patients do not fill their initial prescription for antihypertensive therapy. Adherence is a significant issue with factors contributing to poor adherence being complex and multilevel. A coordinated effort that addresses all barriers is key to improving adherence.
factors: Systematic reviews and meta-analyses have been assessed
Creation of an encouraging and blame-free environment
methods: Use of objective methods (e.g., pill counts, data on medication refills)
Online Data Supplement A and F for more tools
recommendation_support: Once-daily dosing is more beneficial according to meta-analyses

recommendations.recommendation: Effective behavioral and motivational strategies to achieve a healthy lifestyle (i.e., tobacco cessation, weight loss, moderation in alcohol intake, increased physical activity, reduced sodium intake, and consumption of a healthy diet) are recommended for adults with hypertension.

content.synopsis: The primary lifestyle modification interventions that can help reduce high BP include healthy diet, weight loss, exercise, and moderate alcohol intake. Tobacco cessation is crucial for CVD risk reduction. These modifications are essential for good health and require motivational and cognitive intervention strategies to promote adherence. Factors like race, sex, socioeconomic status, and body mass index can affect adherence to lifestyle changes. Interventions like goal setting, feedback, self-monitoring, motivational interviewing, and self-sufficiency promotion are effective when combined. Addressing cultural and social context variables is crucial to influence behavioral change.
in-depth: High-quality evidence supports lifestyle changes for CVD reduction.
Sodium restriction, dietary patterns, weight reduction, and new physical activity habits are beneficial but not always maintained long-term.
Resource-constrained populations face challenges such as access to health care, and the management of hypertension in these areas must address various social and financial constraints.
Strategies involve partnerships with community resources and organizations devoted to hypertension control.

content.recommendation: A team-based care approach is recommended for adults with hypertension.
content.teamBasedCare: Team-based care is a health systems-level, organizational intervention to improve hypertension care quality. It involves roles for multidisciplinary team members such as cardiologists, nurses, pharmacists, and includes technology-based remote monitoring.
content.implementation: Focuses on clinical decision support, prescribed regimen adherence, BP monitoring, and patient self-management.
content.outOfOfficeMonitoring: Out-of-office BP monitoring or no-cost BP control visits are encouraged, particularly in resource-constrained populations.
content.medicationAdherence: Using long-acting once-daily medications and considering longer than 30-day refills are strategies to promote adherence.
content.systemsSupport: Electronic health records and self-management support tools are part of systems-level support.
content.evidence: RCTs and meta-analyses show improvements in SBP and DBP with team-based interventions.

content.guideline_section: 12.3. Health Information Technology-Based Strategies to Promote Hypertension Control

subsections.title: 12.3.1. EHR and Patient Registries

recommendations.details: Use of the EHR and patient registries is beneficial for identification of patients with undiagnosed or undertreated hypertension.
Use of the EHR and patient registries is beneficial for guiding quality improvement efforts designed to improve hypertension control.

subsections.synopsis: A growing number of health systems are using registries and EHR for population health management to identify undiagnosed or undertreated hypertension. Strategies include integrating algorithms to identify at-risk patients, contacting them to schedule BP measurements, providing feedback to clinicians, and using electronic prompts.

subsections.title: 12.3.2. Telehealth Interventions to Improve Hypertension Control

recommendations.details: Telehealth strategies can be useful adjuncts to interventions shown to reduce BP for adults with hypertension.

subsections.synopsis: Telehealth strategies, including telemedicine and mobile health technologies, are promising in managing hypertension with interventions such as linking BP devices to online systems. Wireless technologies and manual data entry are also mentioned as viable components.

content.title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline

sections.title: Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text
details: Meta-analyses of RCTs of different telehealth interventions have demonstrated greater SBP and DBP reductions and a larger proportion of patients achieving BP control than those achieved with usual care without telehealth.
The effect of various telehealth interventions on BP lowering was significantly greater than that of BP self-monitoring without transmission of BP data.
Results are inconsistent with mHealth interventions in reducing SBP in patients with hypertension.

sections.title: 12.4. Improving Quality of Care for Patients With Hypertension

subsections.title: 12.4.1. Performance Measures

recommendations.recommendation: Use of performance measures in combination with other quality improvement strategies at patient-, provider-, and system-based levels is reasonable to facilitate optimal hypertension control.

subsections.synopsis: Efforts to improve suboptimal medical care include the use of performance measures, which are standardized, validated approaches to ensure correct healthcare processes and desired patient outcomes.
subsections.recommendationSpecificSupportiveText: RCTs on the impact of performance measures on hypertension control are lacking; RCTs of quality improvement protocols have shown improvements in hypertension control.

recommendations.recommendation: Use of quality improvement strategies at the health system, provider, and patient levels to improve identification and control of hypertension can be effective.

content.Synopsis: High-quality BP management is multifactorial and requires the engagement of patients, families, healthcare providers, and systems. A disparity exists between treatment success and what is possible with optimal methods, termed a quality gap. Closing this gap is crucial for saving lives. This involves increased patient and healthcare provider awareness, lifestyle changes, access to care, and adherence to medication and follow-ups. Improvement strategies are highlighted for their effectiveness in routine practice for both clinics and communities.
content.Improvement Strategies: These include systematic reviews and trials, identifying substantial reductions in SBP and DBP, and enhancements in BP control with carefully organized interventions at community and healthcare levels.
content.Financial Incentives: Financial incentives improve patient outcomes by rewarding high-quality care over volume. This involves using performance measures and fostering systems like Medicare's Shared Savings Programs. Insurance designs that lower copayments for antihypertensive medications can also facilitate better BP control.
content.Recommendations for Financial Incentives: Financial incentives to providers support better treatment outcomes in hypertensive patients. Health system financing strategies, such as insurance and copayments, improve medication adherence and BP control.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Findings: Population-based payment incentive programs can play an important role in achieving better BP control.

Specific Studies.Outcomes: Reduced copayments for health care and improved outcomes of hypertension care.

Plan of Care for Hypertension.Recommendation: Every adult with hypertension should have a clear, detailed, and evidence-based plan of care.
Goals: Encourages effective management of comorbid conditions.
Prompts timely follow-up with the healthcare team.
Plan of Care for Hypertension.Detailed Elements: Listed in Table 22. Varies among demographic subgroups.

Synopsis.Importance: A specific plan of care for hypertension is essential.
Synopsis.Focus: Understanding modifiable and non-modifiable determinants of health behaviors.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Findings: Implementation of a plan of care can lead to sustained reduction of BP.

Communication Methods: Use of mobile phones and family members for additional communication.

content.General Information: The clinical guideline discusses various aspects of blood pressure management in different contexts such as acute intracerebral hemorrhage, ischemic stroke, secondary stroke prevention, peripheral artery disease, and diabetes mellitus. It includes studies and trials related to the effects of blood pressure control on different health outcomes.

content.title: 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline
content.summary: This document consists of a structured series of guidelines and studies focusing on high blood pressure management, risks, and treatment strategies. It covers various related subjects such as cardiovascular outcomes, intensive blood pressure lowering, and the impact of hypertension in different health conditions like diabetes and chronic kidney disease. The guideline discusses evidence from multiple studies and randomized trials to inform best practices in managing high blood pressure.

Text repeatedly references "ACE inhibitors" and expands ACE as "angiotensin-converting enzyme" as a recommended therapy for hypertension in patients with SIHD and for secondary prevention after MI.
The guideline refers to "ARBs" (angiotensin receptor blockers) as a class of agents recommended for treating hypertension in SIHD; ARBs act on the angiotensin II receptor (commonly AGTR1).
The recommendations list "mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists" (MRAs) as an added therapy option for further BP control; the mineralocorticoid receptor is encoded by NR3C2.
